questions.questionText: Colles fracture is a fracture of the distal radius with:
questions.optionB: Dorsal displacement and angulation of the distal fragment ('dinner fork' deformity — FOOSH injury)
questions.explanation: Colles fracture: fracture of distal radius within 2.5 cm of wrist joint with dorsal displacement/angulation and radial deviation of distal fragment. Mechanism: FOOSH (fall on outstretched hand). Dinner fork deformity (lateral view). Common in elderly (osteoporotic bone). Smith fracture: reverse Colles (volar displacement — fall on dorsum of flexed wrist). Treatment: closed reduction + cast (most cases), ORIF if unstable/intra-articular. Reference: Maheshwari — Colles Fracture.

questions.questionText: The most commonly fractured carpal bone is:
questions.optionB: Scaphoid (risk of avascular necrosis due to retrograde blood supply — blood enters distally)
questions.explanation: Scaphoid: most commonly fractured carpal bone. Mechanism: FOOSH. Anatomical snuffbox tenderness: key clinical finding. Retrograde blood supply (enters distal pole) → proximal pole at risk of AVN (especially waist fractures). Initial X-ray may be normal → repeat in 2 weeks or MRI. Treatment: thumb spica cast (non-displaced), screw fixation (displaced/waist fractures). Complications: AVN (proximal pole), non-union. Reference: Maheshwari — Scaphoid Fracture.

questions.questionText: Anterior dislocation of the shoulder is the most common joint dislocation. The nerve most commonly injured is:
questions.optionB: Axillary nerve (loss of sensation over regimental badge area/deltoid, deltoid weakness)
questions.explanation: Anterior shoulder dislocation (95% of shoulder dislocations): mechanism — abduction, external rotation, extension (ABER). Axillary nerve most commonly injured → test: sensation over regimental badge area (lateral deltoid), deltoid weakness (abduction). Bankart lesion: anteroinferior labral tear (most common pathology). Hill-Sachs lesion: posterolateral humeral head compression fracture. Reduction: Kocher's, Hippocratic, Stimson method. Recurrence high in young patients. Reference: Maheshwari — Shoulder Dislocation.

questions.questionText: Fracture of the neck of femur in elderly patients is best treated by:
questions.optionB: Hemiarthroplasty (displaced, elderly) or total hip replacement (active elderly) — to avoid AVN complications
questions.explanation: Femoral neck fracture: intracapsular fracture. Blood supply (medial circumflex femoral artery) disrupted → high risk of AVN. Garden classification: I-II (undisplaced) → internal fixation (cannulated screws). III-IV (displaced): elderly → hemiarthroplasty (Austin Moore/bipolar), active elderly → total hip replacement. Young patient with displaced fracture: attempt reduction + internal fixation (preserve native hip). Clinical: shortened, externally rotated leg. Reference: Maheshwari — Femoral Neck Fracture.

questions.questionText: Compartment syndrome is diagnosed clinically by:
questions.optionB: Pain out of proportion, pain on passive stretch (earliest and most reliable sign), 5 P's (pain, pressure, paralysis, paresthesia, pulselessness — late)
questions.explanation: Compartment syndrome: increased pressure within closed fascial compartment → compromised tissue perfusion. Pain out of proportion to injury + pain on passive stretch: earliest and most reliable signs. 5 P's (late — tissue damage already occurring): Pain, Pressure (tense swelling), Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pulselessness (very late). Most common site: anterior compartment of leg (after tibial fracture). Diagnosis: clinical (compartment pressure >30 mmHg or within 30 of diastolic confirms). Treatment: EMERGENCY fasciotomy. Reference: Maheshwari — Compartment Syndrome.
